title: Lexing and Parsing
description: Turning source text into structured representations — the front end of every compiler, interpreter, and language tool. Lexing (tokenization) breaks character streams into meaningful tokens. Parsing arranges tokens into trees according to grammar rules.

best_practices.title: Separate lexing from parsing
best_practices.explanation: Lexing converts characters to tokens (handling whitespace, comments, string escapes). Parsing converts tokens to trees (handling grammar rules). Mixing them creates unmaintainable code.

best_practices.title: Use recursive descent for hand-written parsers
best_practices.explanation: Recursive descent maps grammar rules directly to functions: one function per non-terminal, one call per rule reference. The code reads like the grammar.

best_practices.title: Make the lexer produce spans, not just token types
best_practices.explanation: Every token should carry its byte offset (start, end) in the source. This costs almost nothing at lex time but enables precise error messages, IDE go-to-definition, syntax highlighting, and source maps.

gotchas.title: Greedy tokenization breaks on multi-character operators
Left recursion crashes recursive descent parsers
gotchas.bad_example: fn parse_expr() { let left = parse_expr(); expect('+'); }
gotchas.good_example: fn parse_expr() { let mut left = parse_term(); while peek() == '+' { advance(); let right = parse_term(); left = BinOp(left, '+', right); } }

gotchas.title: String escapes in the lexer are a parsing problem
gotchas.bad_example: Scanning until '"' — breaks on escaped quotes
gotchas.good_example: Loop: if char == backslash { skip next char } else if char == quote { break }
